questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Big-O vs Theta — what do you say in an interview? Which response is most accurate?
options: O is upper bound; Θ is tight bound (upper and lower); Ω is lower bound
questions.explanation: Asymptotic notation family: O / Ω / Θ.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Amortized O(1) ArrayList add — meaning? Which response is most accurate?
options: Every single add is ≤ c steps
Average cost per operation over a sequence is O(1), even if some ops are expensive
Worst-case of each add is O(1)
questions.explanation: Amortized ≠ worst-case per operation.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Binary search time complexity on sorted array? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Halving the search space each step.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two nested loops i=0..n−1, j=0..n−1: time? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Θ(n²) iterations. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: for i in 0..n: for j in i..n — complexity? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: ≈ n²/2 iterations → still Θ(n²).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Recursion depth n with O(1) work per frame — stack space? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Call stack grows with depth. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Mergesort time — what do you claim? Which response is most accurate?
options: Θ(n log n) time typical; O(n) extra space for the usual array merge
questions.explanation: Divide-and-conquer merge is n log n with linear extra buffer commonly.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Hash map expected lookup? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Expected constant under good conditions.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When is O(n log n) preferable to O(n²) practically? Which response is most accurate?
options: As soon as n grows — e.g. n=10^5, n² is too slow while n log n is fine
O(n²) is always faster due to smaller constants
questions.explanation: Constraints drive which asymptotics matter.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: log factors: O(n log n) vs O(n) — interview phrasing? Which response is most accurate?
options: log n is negligible so they are identical always
Same linearithmic vs linear class; for huge n, log n still grows — but often acceptable
log n means log base 10 only
questions.explanation: Be honest: slower than linear, usually OK vs quadratic.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Best / average / worst case — why distinguish? Which response is most accurate?
options: Only worst case exists in Big-O
Same algorithm can differ (e.g. Quicksort); state which you mean
Average case is illegal to mention
questions.explanation: Clarify the case analysis. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Dropping constants: is 3n² + 7n O(n²)? Which response is most accurate?
options: Yes — leading term dominates; constants ignored in Big-O
questions.explanation: Asymptotics ignore constant factors and lower terms.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Linear search worst-case? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: May scan all elements. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Space complexity of storing an n×n matrix densely? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: n² entries. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Tail recursion — complexity angle? Which response is most accurate?
options: Always uses O(n) stack even when optimized
Can reuse frames → O(1) stack if language optimizes TCO; Java generally does not
questions.explanation: Know TCO vs languages that don't do it.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Comparing 2^n vs n! growth? Which response is most accurate?
options: They are Θ of each other
n! grows faster than 2^n for large n
questions.explanation: Factorial outpaces exponential base 2.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: O(1) space meaning? Which response is most accurate?
options: Extra memory independent of n (aside from input); few variables
questions.explanation: Auxiliary space doesn't grow with input size.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why say 'expected O(n)' for Quickselect/hash? Which response is most accurate?
options: Randomization / average over inputs or pivots; not a hard worst-case guarantee
It is the same as amortized
questions.explanation: Probabilistic/average guarantees ≠ worst-case.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Nested loop where inner runs 1,2,4,...,n (powers of two)? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Geometric series of inner work sums to O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Screening: sorting comparison-based lower bound? Which response is most accurate?
options: Ω(n log n) in the algebraic decision-tree model
questions.explanation: Information-theoretic lower bound for general comparison sorting.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Master theorem vibe: T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) — class? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Same as mergesort recurrence. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: DFS on a graph adjacency list — time? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Each vertex/edge processed constantly often.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Binary heap insert complexity? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Bubble up height of tree. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Building a heap from n elements via heapify? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Linear-time heap construction is a classic result.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Two pointers on sorted array for 2-sum — time? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Each pointer moves at most n times.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Sliding window maximum with deque — time? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Each index enters/leaves deque once.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Dijkstra with binary heap on adjacency list? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Heap operations dominate. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why count sort / radix can beat Ω(n log n)? Which response is most accurate?
options: They assume integer/digit structure — not pure comparison sorts
questions.explanation: Model matters: comparison lower bound doesn't apply.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Backtracking complexity talk track? Which response is most accurate?
options: Often exponential in worst case; prune to make practical — state the branching
questions.explanation: Be explicit about search tree size.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: DP time for classic LCS lengths n,m? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Fill n×m table. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Amortized analysis example beyond ArrayList? Which response is most accurate?
options: Splay trees / union-find (almost O(1) α(n)) / dynamic table
questions.explanation: Several classic amortized structures.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Union-find with path compression + union by rank? Which response is most accurate?
options: O(log log log n) worst each without amortize
questions.explanation: Near-constant amortized. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: String hashing all substrings naively? Which response is most accurate?
options: O(n²) hashes if each computed from scratch over its span
questions.explanation: Unless rolling/prefix hashes, substring hashing blows up.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Comparing nested loops: j from 0 to i vs 0 to n? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Sum of i is quadratic. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: Memory locality can make O(n²) array code beat O(n log n) pointer-heavy code for moderate n — true?
options: False; asymptotics always predict wall clock
True — constants and caches matter; still discuss asymptotics for scaling
questions.explanation: Practical performance ≠ only Big-O; scaling still matters.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: BFS shortest path unweighted graph complexity? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: Queue processes each vertex/edge constantly.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Potentially better: O(n) hash Two Sum vs O(n log n) sort+pointers — catch? Which answer is best?
options: Hash uses extra memory and has worse constants/worst-case; sort is deterministic
Hash always wins on memory too
questions.explanation: Trade space, determinism, and constants.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Recurrence T(n)=T(n−1)+O(n) — closed form? Which response is most accurate?
questions.explanation: 1+2+…+n = Θ(n²). State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Log base in Big-O for algorithms — does base matter? Which response is most accurate?
options: Yes, log2 vs log10 changes the class
No — logs differ by a constant factor, absorbed in Big-O
questions.explanation: Change-of-base is a constant. State this clearly and mention complexity when relevant.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Online vs offline algorithms — complexity relevance? Which response is most accurate?
options: Online processes input as it arrives (constraints on future knowledge); can change feasible complexity/approach
questions.explanation: Problem model affects what algorithms are allowed.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Senior: amortized vs expected — crisp difference? Which response is most accurate?
options: Amortized: average over a sequence of ops (often deterministic accounting); expected: average over random choices/inputs
questions.explanation: Don't conflate accounting arguments with probability.

questions.stem: Cache-oblivious / practical: why Θ(n) sequential scan ≪ Θ(n) random probes?
options: Prefetch and cache lines favor sequential access; same asymptotics, different constants/hardware
Sequential scan is O(log n) on CPUs
questions.explanation: Hardware reality under the asymptotics.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Pitfall: claiming O(1) for HashMap with string keys of length m? Which response is most accurate?
options: Hashing/equals may cost O(m); say O(m) expected per op w.r.t. key length
questions.explanation: Key size is part of the model.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Persistent data structures — complexity tradeoff theme? Which response is most accurate?
options: Persistence reduces time below ephemeral structures always
Structural sharing can make updates O(log n) while preserving old versions
questions.explanation: Versioned updates cost logarithmic structure sharing typically.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: When O(n²) DP is acceptable in interviews? Which response is most accurate?
options: When n≤~300–1000 depending on limits; confirm constraints before optimizing prematurely
When interviewer forbids O(n log n)
questions.explanation: Match algorithm to constraints.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Lower bound arguments — how to use lightly in interview? Which response is most accurate?
options: E.g. must read all input ⇒ Ω(n); comparison sort ⇒ Ω(n log n) — shows your solution is optimal in model
questions.explanation: Simple adversary/information arguments impress when accurate.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Soft O / ignoring log^* / α(n) — honest senior take? Which response is most accurate?
options: Some factors grow so slowly they're treated as constant practically, but say so explicitly
questions.explanation: Communicate practical vs theoretical growth.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Parallelism: work vs span (depth) — why mention? Which response is most accurate?
options: Total operations (work) vs critical-path length (span) describe parallel speedup limits
questions.explanation: Modern systems interviews may touch parallel complexity.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Integer addition of two n-bit numbers — complexity? Which response is most accurate?
options: O(n) bit operations in multi-tape / bit models; word RAM may treat word ops O(1)
questions.explanation: Model (word RAM vs bit) changes the answer.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Premature micro-optimization vs Big-O — balance? Which response is most accurate?
options: Pick the right asymptotic class for constraints first; profile constants only when needed
questions.explanation: Right algorithm first, then measure.
